header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence
header.stance: Oil lifted inflation risk and real yields together, tightening rates, AI risk and external funding.

overall_summary.headline: The energy shock raised both inflation compensation and the real discount rate.
overall_summary.summary: An 11% oil surge drove a belly-led Treasury selloff while real yields and breakevens both rose. Semiconductors, listed credit and EM debt weakened as the market raised the policy path and the cost of financing capital-intensive growth.
overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: Renewed U.S.-Iran fighting and uncertainty over Hormuz lifted oil, inflation compensation and the expected Fed path. The 5Y led Treasury losses, while higher real yields transmitted the shock into semiconductors, gold, credit and EM debt.

watch_events.title: CPI And Warsh Testimony
watch_events.watch_line: Core inflation and the Fed reaction function determine whether oil becomes a broader policy shock.
watch_events.market_trigger: Core CPI composition and Warsh's treatment of energy pass-through and inflation expectations.
watch_events.market_implication: Persistent core pressure would extend the belly selloff, while softer core inflation could separate energy from the policy path.
watch_events.confirms_if: The 2Y, 5Y and real yields rise with firm core inflation and restrictive testimony.
watch_events.challenges_if: Core inflation softens and real yields reverse despite elevated oil.
watch_events.why_it_matters: It is the nearest test of whether the close was an energy event or a wider inflation regime shift.

watch_events.title: PPI And Beige Book
watch_events.watch_line: Pipeline costs and regional pricing power test the persistence behind CPI.
watch_events.market_trigger: Core PPI, energy-sensitive inputs and evidence that firms can pass costs through.
watch_events.market_implication: Confirmation keeps real rates restrictive and raises credit concession risk.
watch_events.confirms_if: Producer prices and business pricing power remain firm.
watch_events.challenges_if: Input costs ease as regional demand and pricing power weaken.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: UST belly, real yields and investment-grade credit
watch_events.why_it_matters: It distinguishes one-day oil repricing from a durable cost pipeline.

watch_events.title: Retail Sales And Claims
watch_events.watch_line: Demand and labor decide whether the economy can absorb tighter real rates.
watch_events.market_trigger: Retail control-group momentum and the direction of initial and continuing claims.
watch_events.market_implication: Resilient activity validates policy restraint, while weaker demand broadens duration relief.
watch_events.confirms_if: Sales stay firm and claims remain contained.
watch_events.challenges_if: Consumption weakens while claims begin to rise.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Front-end Treasuries, high yield and cyclicals
watch_events.why_it_matters: It separates inflation-led tightening from a broader growth slowdown.

watch_events.title: Treasury Bill Liquidity
watch_events.watch_line: Bill auctions measure cash demand across the inflation and policy event window.
watch_events.market_trigger: Bid-to-cover, stop levels and concessions in 17-week, 4-week, 8-week, 3-month and 6-month supply.
watch_events.market_implication: Strong demand cushions front-end liquidity, while weak clearing reinforces the higher policy path.
watch_events.confirms_if: Bills clear with deep demand and contained concessions.
watch_events.challenges_if: Cash investors demand materially higher yields through the event sequence.
watch_events.why_it_matters: Liquidity determines whether event premium remains orderly.

watch_events.title: BOK And Friday Macro Checks
watch_events.watch_line: Asian policy, housing, production and inflation expectations complete the global test.
watch_events.market_trigger: BOK guidance, housing starts, industrial production and Michigan inflation expectations.
watch_events.market_implication: The sequence tests imported energy inflation, rate-sensitive demand and credibility.
watch_events.confirms_if: Policy remains credible while expectations stay contained and activity avoids a sharp break.
watch_events.challenges_if: Currency pressure or higher expectations force a more restrictive global rates path.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Asian front ends, UST duration and breakevens
watch_events.why_it_matters: It shows whether the U.S. move sits inside a wider policy tightening impulse.

cross_asset_cards.title: Oil
cross_asset_cards.summary: Higher crude and firmer breakevens pushed against duration.

cross_asset_cards.title: Credit / EM
cross_asset_cards.summary: Duration, dollar strength and imported-energy risk weakened funded assets while borrower and sovereign quality still set the distribution.

cross_asset_cards.title: Dollar / gold
cross_asset_cards.summary: Treasury yields were higher, and dollar firmness tightened financial conditions.

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

The July 13 close converted renewed Middle East fighting into a broad increase in the price of inflation, policy risk and capital. WTI and Brent each rose about 11%, and Treasuries cheapened from the front end through the long bond. The 5-year yield led with a 7-basis-point rise to 4.37%, followed by the 10-year at 4.62%, up 6 basis points, the 2-year at 4.26%, up 5 basis points, and the 30-year at 5.10%, up 4 basis points. The shape was not a simple energy-driven steepening. The belly underperformed, the 10-year real yield rose 4 basis points to 2.36%, and the 10-year breakeven rose 2 basis points to 2.26%. Investors demanded more compensation for inflation while also raising the real discount rate and the expected policy path.

The risk board confirmed that this was a tightening shock rather than a narrow commodity move. The S&P 500 fell 0.79%, the Nasdaq 100 lost 1.88%, and semiconductors dropped 4.78%, while the VIX rose 14.17%. LQD declined 0.47%, high-yield exchange-traded funds weakened, and EMB fell 0.64% as DXY gained 0.31%. Gold dropped 2.46% even as geopolitical risk intensified, an important sign that higher real yields and a firmer dollar outweighed immediate demand for an inflation hedge. The close raised financing costs for duration, growth and external-dollar borrowers at the same time.

Oil supplied the direct transmission channel. Renewed U.S.-Iran strikes and conflicting claims over the Strait of Hormuz restored concern that physical supply and shipping could remain impaired. The market therefore repriced more than the spot cost of crude. It raised the probability that energy would persist in consumer and producer prices, that the Federal Reserve would have less room to tolerate above-target inflation, and that import-dependent economies would face renewed currency and balance-of-payments pressure. Local reporting indicated that traders had moved close to fully pricing a September rate increase, compared with roughly two-thirds probability a week earlier. That estimate is contextual rather than a checked market row, but it matches the front-end and real-yield direction.

AI risk made the financial transmission more concentrated. SK Hynix's sharp fall in Seoul spilled into U.S. trading after its record $26.5 billion listing, while the semiconductor index registered the largest loss among the main checked equity gauges. The underlying demand story did not disappear: TSMC reported 36% quarterly sales growth, and hyperscalers continue to commit large sums to data centers. The tension lies in financing. Unsecured investment-grade issuance has carried much of the buildout, but investor absorption is approaching practical limits, increasing the need for tenant-backed data-center bonds, private credit and other structures. Higher real yields and weaker technology equity prices make that transition more expensive.

Global duration and EM credit did not offer a clean offset. Japan stepped back from expectations of an immediate GPIF allocation shift toward domestic assets, weakening the yen and JGB support created by last week's comments. Euro-area growth forecasts were cut as the energy shock revived a stagflationary mix. Indonesia retained investment grade and Chile found a favorable issuance window, yet Kenya, Nigeria and Ethiopia showed why liability management and inflation credibility still govern access. The market remains open, but the distribution of funding conditions is widening.

The next decision point arrives quickly. CPI and Chairman Kevin Warsh's testimony on July 14 will determine whether the oil-led selloff is reinforced by persistent core inflation and a less patient reaction function. PPI, the Beige Book and a second day of testimony follow on July 15. Retail sales and claims on July 16 then test whether demand and labor can sustain restrictive real rates. The close leaves fixed income with a clear hierarchy: separate the energy impulse from core persistence, observe whether real yields hold above 2.3%, and watch whether primary credit and EM funding can clear without larger concessions.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

The Treasury selloff was broad but concentrated in intermediate maturities. The 2-year yield closed at 4.26%, up 5 basis points; the 5-year at 4.37%, up 7 basis points; the 10-year at 4.62%, up 6 basis points; and the 30-year at 5.10%, up 4 basis points. The 2s10s curve steepened 1 basis point to 36 basis points, while 2s30s flattened 1 basis point to 84 basis points. Those small curve changes conceal a larger outright adjustment. Investors raised the expected policy path and the real rate applied to the next several years, while the long bond already carried substantial compensation above 5%.

Curvature makes the belly pressure visible. The 2s5s10s butterfly rose 3 basis points to minus 14 basis points, and the 2s10s30s butterfly rose 3 basis points to minus 12 basis points. The 5-year sector underperformed the wings because it sits where a delayed or renewed tightening cycle has the most direct cumulative effect. A pure fiscal bear steepening would have pushed the 30-year more aggressively. A pure near-term inflation scare would have concentrated the move in bills and the 2-year. Instead, the market lifted yields through the policy horizon and retained an already-high long-end term premium.

The real-yield and breakeven split shows why. The 10-year TIPS yield rose 4 basis points to 2.36%, while the 10-year breakeven increased 2 basis points to 2.26%. Five- and seven-year real yields also rose 4 basis points, and long-dated real yields increased 3 basis points. Energy restored inflation compensation, but the larger real-yield move says investors also expected a firmer policy response and a higher equilibrium burden on investment. This was a combined inflation and real-rate shock, not merely an oil premium added to unchanged financial conditions.

Bills recorded the same event sensitivity in uneven form. The 13-week coupon-equivalent yield rose 5 basis points to 3.85%, the 26-week rose 4 basis points to 4.00%, and the 52-week rose 6 basis points to 4.12%. Very short 4-, 6- and 8-week yields moved little. Cash instruments closest to the current policy setting remained anchored, while maturities extending through the prospective tightening window demanded more compensation. That pattern supports the interpretation that investors repriced the coming policy path rather than current overnight funding stress.

The Fed communication problem now matters as much as the headline rate forecast. Warsh has resisted conventional forward guidance, and market participants are asking for a clearer explanation of how inflation, labor and growth map into policy. The absence of a fixed roadmap increases the information content of each release. It also amplifies the role of testimony: investors will listen for whether the chairman treats the oil shock as a temporary relative-price move, a source of second-round inflation, or a reason to tighten before expectations become less anchored.

CPI will separate those possibilities. A firm core reading would validate the real-yield and belly selloff independently of energy. A softer core print with high headline inflation would leave a more complicated configuration: breakevens could remain supported while policy-sensitive real yields find relief. If both headline and core surprise higher, the 2-year and 5-year can extend their rise and the curve may flatten more decisively. If core inflation softens and Warsh emphasizes patience, the 5-year's underperformance becomes vulnerable to reversal.

PPI and the Beige Book provide the second layer of confirmation. Producer prices will show whether energy and supply disruption are entering the cost pipeline. The Beige Book will reveal whether businesses retain the pricing power to pass those costs through and whether activity remains strong enough to absorb restrictive financing. Retail sales and claims then test the demand side. Strong consumption and stable labor would support higher real yields; weaker demand or rising claims would argue that the market has tightened enough even if headline inflation stays elevated.

Bill auctions around the releases give a separate liquidity reading. The 17-week sale on July 15 and 4- and 8-week auctions on July 16 will show whether cash demand remains deep through the data window. Strong bidding would limit the risk that event premium becomes a funding problem. Weak clearing would reinforce the rise in intermediate bill yields and show that cash investors require more compensation for policy uncertainty. The key rates conclusion is therefore conditional but precise: the market has priced a higher path, and the next data must decide whether that path is core-inflation persistence or an energy-driven event premium.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Credit weakened in a pattern consistent with higher Treasury yields and a broader risk-off move. LQD fell 0.47%, reflecting its longer duration as well as spread sensitivity. HYG lost 0.24% and JNK 0.18%, smaller declines that show the session did not become a generalized default scare. The listed gauges mark a market that remains open but charges more for duration and lower-quality exposure. Confirmation now comes from books, concessions, structure and secondary trading rather than from a single ETF close.

AI infrastructure is the largest absorption test. Hyperscalers have relied heavily on unsecured investment-grade bonds to fund data centers, chips and power capacity. Investors have supported that strategy because the borrowers have strong cash flows and strategic assets, but local reporting shows concern that unsecured demand cannot absorb the full scale indefinitely. Data-center bonds are emerging as an alternative, shifting the underwriting focus toward tenant payment capacity, lease structure, collateral and project execution. Private credit and equity-linked financing provide other channels, yet each carries a different liquidity and risk premium.

The July 13 semiconductor selloff raises the price of that diversification. SK Hynix shares suffered a record decline in Seoul and its U.S. receipts weakened after the largest foreign-company U.S. listing, while the checked semiconductor index fell almost 4.8%. TSMC's 36% sales growth confirms that underlying hardware demand remains strong, so the market is not rejecting AI as an economic theme. It is repricing valuation, concentration and the balance-sheet burden required to deliver the buildout. Lower equity values can reduce the cushion supporting convertible or equity-linked structures at the same time that higher real yields increase debt costs.

Meta's expected spending of roughly $40 billion on its Louisiana campus illustrates the scale. Projects of that size cannot rely on one investor base without eventually changing price or structure. The next phase of financing is likely to distribute exposure across corporate bonds, project-style securities, private lenders, infrastructure capital and potentially asset-backed channels. That broadening is constructive when it creates durable matching between long-lived assets and long-duration investors. It becomes restrictive when borrowers need larger concessions, tighter covenants or more collateral to complete transactions.

Pinewood offers a smaller but cleaner primary-market observable. The studio is meeting investors about a potential sterling fixed-rate issue, subject to market conditions. It has an existing £750 million bond due in 2030 with a 6% coupon and carries split ratings of BB- from S&P and BBB- from Fitch. Demand for the new transaction can show whether sterling investors will absorb crossover or high-yield risk after the global rates selloff. Strong books and orderly secondary trading would support the view that access remains available beyond the largest technology borrowers. Wider pricing or a delayed deal would show that the shock is narrowing the window.

The credit confidence ladder remains positive but disciplined. Investment-grade issuers with strong cash flow can fund, although duration exposure makes their bonds sensitive to Treasuries. High yield remains tradable, but commodity, refinancing and earnings risk matter more when real yields rise. AI projects can attract capital, but the underwriting is moving from corporate reputation toward tenant contracts, collateral and construction execution. The narrow conclusion is not that credit is closed. It is that the marginal lender requires clearer protection and a better-defined source of repayment.

The next confirmation should come from primary execution and secondary resilience. Stable LQD after CPI would show that Treasury duration is no longer overwhelming investment-grade carry. HYG and JNK holding their levels would indicate that the shock has not migrated into broad default concern. Pinewood and any AI-linked financing should be judged by order-book breadth, final spread, maturity and post-pricing performance. If those channels clear cleanly, the July 13 move remains a repricing of capital rather than a funding break. If concessions widen and secondary prices weaken, the oil shock will have reached credit more deeply than the listed losses alone suggest.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Japan reversed part of the previous session's home-bias thesis. Reports that the government had no plan to change GPIF's strategic allocation weakened the yen by as much as 0.4% to 162.36 per dollar and extended JGB losses. The $1.8 trillion pension fund currently divides its portfolio evenly among domestic bonds, domestic equities, foreign bonds and foreign equities. Last week's call for pensions to invest more at home had encouraged expectations of stronger domestic demand and less structural overseas allocation. The denial restores a more cautious baseline: verbal support for domestic assets has not yet become a portfolio-policy change.

That distinction matters for global duration. Japan remains a major source of overseas savings, but the July 13 evidence does not justify assuming immediate repatriation or reduced Treasury buying. Investors must now separate political jawboning aimed at the yen and JGB market from an actual change in GPIF benchmarks. The yen and long JGB yields will remain sensitive to any formal review, yet the current allocation framework leaves global markets competing for Japanese capital on existing terms.

Europe faced a different duration problem. Economists cut the 2026 euro-area growth forecast to 0.5% from 0.7%, below the ECB's 0.8% baseline, as renewed Hormuz disruption weakened hopes for normal energy shipments. Their median inflation forecast remained 2.8%, well above the ECB's target. The combination is stagflationary: weaker activity would normally support duration, but persistent energy inflation limits the central bank's room to ease. European curves must price both slower growth and a reaction function constrained by imported prices.

The global mechanism therefore runs through both savings allocation and inflation exposure. Japan's immediate home-bias impulse faded because no strategic GPIF change was planned. Europe lost growth momentum but retained inflation pressure because the energy shock is external and difficult to offset. Neither channel provides a straightforward cushion for the U.S. long end. Instead, they add competition for inflation protection, currency hedging and sovereign duration at a time when the U.S. 30-year yield is already above 5%.

The Bank of Korea decision on July 16 is the nearest regional policy test. Korea must balance energy-import costs, currency stability and domestic financial conditions after extraordinary volatility in semiconductor equities. A more restrictive signal would support the view that the oil shock is pushing global policy paths higher. A patient stance paired with currency stability would show that local financial conditions are already doing enough work. The result matters for Asian front ends and dollar funding, but it should not be folded backward into the July 13 U.S. close.

Fiscal supply remains a secondary constraint. Sovereigns still need to fund defense, energy transition and ordinary deficits while investors demand compensation for volatile inflation. The immediate article does not require a standalone supply section because oil and policy repricing explain the close more directly. The relevant global-duration conclusion is that high long-end yields are not creating one synchronized buying opportunity. Allocation rules, currency pressure, inflation exposure and fiscal credibility continue to determine where demand appears.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

EM entered the close with a tighter common backdrop. DXY rose 0.31% and EMB fell 0.64%, a larger decline than either high-yield ETF. Oil's 11% rise increases import costs for many sovereigns, while higher U.S. real yields raise the opportunity cost of holding external debt. That combination does not close the market indiscriminately, but it raises the premium on fiscal anchors, reserve adequacy and active liability management.

Indonesia retained its investment-grade rating and stable outlook from S&P, supported by the expectation that the statutory 3% budget-deficit ceiling will remain an anchor. The decision provides a credible floor after the June bond selloff driven by interventionist-policy concerns and higher-rate expectations. Indonesian bonds have still produced a 10% hedged dollar loss this year, showing that a stable rating cannot eliminate market volatility. It does establish what investors need to see: fiscal discipline maintained across administrations even as spending priorities expand.

Chile occupies the stronger end of the external-funding ladder. The spread on its dollar bonds has tightened to about 82 basis points over Treasuries, near levels last seen in 2007, and the Senate authorized an additional $6.2 billion of borrowing, most expected abroad. Tight spreads and strong foreign demand create an issuance window despite weak domestic growth and Middle East risk. The market is rewarding institutional quality and access, but the upcoming sale must still clear without giving back the valuation advantage.

Kenya is using liability management rather than a pure new-money story. Officials are considering buying back as much as $500 million of eurobonds while issuing new dollar debt to extend maturities. It would be the fourth buyback in two years. The strategy can reduce near-term repayment pressure if the new issue prices at sustainable terms and the maturity extension is meaningful. It can also become expensive if higher U.S. yields or oil keep spreads from tightening. Execution, not the announcement, determines whether the transaction improves the debt profile.

Nigeria and Ethiopia show the domestic-policy side of the ladder. Standard Chartered expects Nigeria's easing cycle to be slower because inflation expectations remain elevated, with the policy rate still 26.5% and projected to end 2026 near 25%. Ethiopia raised its policy rate to 16% from 15% while removing a credit-growth ceiling, pairing tighter price control with a more market-based allocation of lending. Both cases signal that local authorities are prioritizing credibility under inflation pressure, even though high domestic rates constrain growth and bank borrowers.

Argentina remains politically sensitive, but its immediate value is as reserve context rather than a close driver. The market assigns large consequences to whether future policy preserves fiscal discipline and debt repayment, yet July 13 price action was better explained by oil, U.S. rates and the dollar. Keeping that distinction prevents political background from becoming false same-day causality.

The EM conclusion is a selective one. Indonesia marks the value of a fiscal rule, Chile marks strong market access, Kenya marks proactive maturity management, and Nigeria and Ethiopia mark inflation credibility through restraint. Higher oil and U.S. real yields narrow the margin for error across all five. Confirmation will come from local currencies, auction and syndication demand, and whether EMB stabilizes after CPI. A stronger dollar, further oil gains or weaker books would push the common backdrop from selective tightening toward broader stress.

## Cross Asset

Oil was the session's organizing cross-asset force. WTI closed at 79.27 and Brent at 84.38, each up about 11%, after renewed strikes and disagreement over whether Hormuz remained open. The size of the move restored a physical-supply premium that had faded during the previous period of calm. Because the strait is central to global energy shipping, uncertainty over access affects more than crude inventories. It changes freight, insurance, refined-product availability and the time required for lower energy prices to reach consumers.

Equities transmitted the shock through duration and concentration. The S&P 500 fell 0.79%, the Nasdaq 100 lost 1.88%, and the semiconductor index dropped 4.78%. Volatility rose 14.17% to 17.16. The larger losses in technology and chips show that investors were reducing exposure to long-duration earnings and crowded AI positions as real yields rose. SK Hynix's record decline and weaker U.S. receipts intensified that move, even while TSMC's sales growth showed that end demand remained robust.

Gold's 2.46% decline to 4,003 and DXY's 0.31% gain sharpen the interpretation. Geopolitical risk did not automatically create a precious-metals rally because higher real yields and a stronger dollar raised the carrying cost of gold. The 10-year real yield at 2.36% is a powerful competing safe asset. The result was an inflation shock expressed through oil and breakevens, combined with real-rate tightening that pressured gold and growth equities.

Credit and EM confirmed the funding dimension. LQD lost 0.47%, HYG and JNK declined modestly, and EMB fell 0.64%. The distribution is coherent: long-duration investment grade and external sovereign debt were more sensitive to higher Treasury yields and dollar strength, while high yield registered a smaller immediate move. This is a confidence ladder rather than a proof caveat. Investment-grade duration marked the rate shock, high yield marked contained but weaker risk appetite, and EMB marked the added external-liquidity burden.

The close should remain separate from later Asian trading. WTI extended its advance after New York, and Asian Stock-index futures remained weak, but those developments belong to the next observable. They can confirm or challenge the July 13 mechanism without rewriting it. The checked close already establishes the transmission: higher oil raised inflation and policy pricing, real yields tightened, AI equities weakened, volatility rose, and funded risk softened.

The next cross-asset test is whether oil holds the gap after CPI. If crude remains elevated and core CPI is firm, rates, DXY and credit can tighten together. If oil retreats while core inflation softens, breakevens and the front end should find relief even if geopolitical uncertainty persists. Semiconductors provide a separate risk-appetite gauge: stabilization would show that investors still distinguish strong AI demand from financing-cost pressure, while further weakness would indicate that valuation and capital intensity are becoming the dominant market view.

## Forward Market Significance

Core and headline CPI on July 14 are the first and largest tests. The market enters the release with higher breakevens, a 2.36% 10-year real yield and a belly-led Treasury selloff. A firm core print would show that policy pressure extends beyond energy and validate the higher 2-year and 5-year yields. Softer core inflation alongside a high headline reading would split the response, supporting breakevens while allowing some real-yield relief. The decisive market confirmation is not one number but the combination of core composition, real yields and the front-end curve.

Warsh's testimony later on July 14 and again July 15 owns the reaction-function question. Investors need to know how the chairman distinguishes a temporary energy shock from second-round inflation and how much patience remains when market-based policy expectations have moved higher. A clear framework could reduce event premium even if the policy stance remains firm. Continued reluctance to describe the reaction function would leave data releases carrying more of the burden and keep volatility elevated.

PPI on July 15 is the pipeline test. Producer prices can reveal whether energy, freight and supply disruption are becoming broader input-cost pressure. The Beige Book later that day adds evidence on pricing power and demand across regions. Firm PPI with strong business pricing would reinforce the real-yield and credit selloff. Softer pipeline prices with weaker activity would support duration, though credit could then face a growth rather than inflation concern.

Retail sales and claims on July 16 rank next because they determine whether demand and labor can absorb restrictive policy. Strong retail spending and contained claims would make it harder for the Fed to look through an upside inflation surprise. Weak sales or rising claims would create a more balanced path and could cap real yields. The favorable credit outcome is moderate demand with stable employment; a sharp deterioration would help Treasuries while challenging lower-quality spreads.

Bill auctions provide the liquidity overlay. The 17-week sale on July 15 and 4- and 8-week sales on July 16 will measure cash demand around the releases. Strong bidding and contained concessions would show that front-end liquidity remains deep. Weak clearing would validate the rise in intermediate bill yields and extend event premium. Three- and six-month auctions on July 20 complete the seven-day horizon.

The Bank of Korea decision is the principal global-policy observable. Korea's exposure to imported energy, currency pressure and volatile technology equities makes the decision a direct test of the same shock through a different economy. A restrictive signal would reinforce the global policy channel; patience with stable currency markets would suggest local financial conditions are sufficient. Friday's housing starts, industrial production and Michigan inflation expectations then test rate-sensitive demand, supply momentum and credibility.

The market enters the sequence with clear thresholds. Rates need softer core inflation or weaker demand to reverse the belly selloff. Credit needs stable Treasuries and clean primary books to show that access remains broad. Global duration needs clarity on Japan's allocation policy and contained European energy inflation. EM needs stable DXY, resilient local currencies and successful Chilean or Kenyan execution. If those confirmations arrive together, July 13 can remain a bounded energy shock. If oil, core inflation and concessions all rise, the close becomes the start of a wider tightening cycle.

section_summaries.summary: An 11% oil surge raised inflation compensation, real yields and the expected Fed path together. Treasuries cheapened most in the belly, while semiconductors, volatility, credit and EM debt confirmed a broader tightening of capital conditions. AI financing, euro-area stagflation and selective sovereign access widened the consequences beyond energy ahead of CPI and Warsh testimony.
section_summaries.market_read: Oil became a combined inflation, policy and capital-cost shock.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The market tightened both nominal funding and the real discount rate applied to growth.
section_summaries.next_check: CPI composition and Warsh's reaction function.

section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: The 5Y led a broad Treasury selloff as the market raised the cumulative policy path through the belly. The 10Y real yield rose 4 basis points and breakevens added 2 basis points, showing that energy inflation and real-rate restraint moved together. Intermediate bills also cheapened, leaving CPI, Fed testimony and auction demand to decide whether event premium persists.
section_summaries.market_read: The belly absorbed both higher inflation compensation and a firmer policy path.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: A combined real-yield and breakeven move tightens more channels than a pure oil premium.
section_summaries.next_check: Core CPI, Warsh testimony and bill clearing.

section_summaries.summary: Listed credit weakened without signaling a broad default shock, but the financing hurdle rose for long-duration and capital-intensive borrowers. AI infrastructure is pushing funding beyond unsecured hyperscaler bonds toward tenant-backed and private structures as absorption limits emerge. Pinewood's potential sterling issue offers a concrete test of crossover demand, concessions and secondary performance after the rates selloff.
section_summaries.market_read: Funding remains open, but structure and investor protection now carry a higher price.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: AI demand can remain strong while the capital required to deliver it becomes harder to place.
section_summaries.next_check: Primary books, concessions and LQD stabilization.

section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Japan denied plans for an immediate GPIF allocation change, reversing part of the yen and JGB support created by home-bias expectations. Europe faced the opposite pressure as economists cut growth while retaining above-target inflation after renewed Hormuz disruption. Global duration therefore lacked a synchronized relief channel, with allocation policy, imported energy and currency stability setting local outcomes.
section_summaries.market_read: Japan's savings allocation stayed unchanged while Europe absorbed a stagflationary energy shock.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Neither channel supplied a clean offset to high U.S. long-end yields.
section_summaries.next_check: Yen and JGB stability plus the BOK decision.

section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: A firmer dollar, higher oil and weaker EMB tightened the common backdrop, but sovereign quality still determined access. Indonesia retained investment grade through its fiscal anchor, Chile held a favorable external-issuance window, and Kenya pursued a maturity-extending buyback. Nigeria and Ethiopia kept policy restrictive, showing that credibility and liability management remain the positive confidence ladder for EM debt.
section_summaries.market_read: EM access survived where fiscal anchors and active debt management offset the external shock.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Higher energy and real yields reduce the margin for weak institutions and passive refinancing.
section_summaries.next_check: Local FX, Chile issuance and Kenya buyback terms.

section_summaries.summary: Oil organized the cross-asset move as renewed Hormuz uncertainty lifted inflation risk and Treasury yields. Technology and semiconductors underperformed, volatility rose, and funded risk weakened. Gold fell despite geopolitical tension because higher real yields and a firmer dollar dominated immediate hedge demand, confirming that the shock tightened both inflation compensation and the real price of capital.
section_summaries.market_read: Energy inflation and real-rate tightening reinforced each other across risk assets.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The configuration can pressure growth, credit and external borrowers simultaneously.
section_summaries.next_check: Oil persistence, SOX stabilization and DXY follow-through.

section_summaries.summary: CPI and Warsh testimony are the nearest tests of whether oil becomes persistent policy pressure. PPI and the Beige Book then assess pipeline costs and pricing power, followed by retail sales and claims for demand and labor. Bill auctions measure cash liquidity around the releases, while BOK, housing, production and Michigan expectations complete the seven-day global check.
section_summaries.market_read: Inflation comes first, followed by policy interpretation, demand and liquidity confirmation.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The sequence determines whether July 13 remains bounded or begins a wider tightening cycle.
section_summaries.next_check: CPI, Warsh, PPI, retail sales and claims.

methodology_note: Synthesized market analysis from market levels and reporting context. Forward Market Significance carries the next market checks.
